learning_objectives: Apply psychoanalytic, behavioral, cognitive-behavioral, and humanistic theories in nursing care.
Incorporate milieu therapy principles into practice.

sections.title: Overview of Conceptual Models
sections.content: Psychiatric nursing draws on multiple theories to explain behavior and design treatment. Appreciating each model\u2019s strengths enables nurses to choose appropriate interventions. This overview sets the stage for the models described in later sections.

sections.title: Psychoanalytic Theory
sections.content: Freud described the id, ego, and superego as well as defense mechanisms and unconscious conflicts. Psychoanalytic therapy seeks to uncover hidden thoughts through techniques like free association and dream analysis. Nurses apply awareness of defense mechanisms in everyday care.

sections.title: Erikson’s Developmental Stages
sections.content: Erikson expanded psychodynamic theory with eight developmental stages from infancy to old age. Each stage presents tasks such as identity versus role confusion that shape relationships and personality. Understanding these tasks guides nursing interventions across the lifespan.

sections.title: Peplau’s Interpersonal Theory
sections.content: Peplau emphasized the therapeutic nurse–client relationship. Her phases of orientation, working, and termination structure communication and promote collaboration. Roles such as teacher, counselor, and resource person help clients build trust and skills.

sections.title: Behavioral Therapy Principles
sections.content: Behavioral therapy focuses on observable actions. Techniques like systematic desensitization, token economies, and positive reinforcement help replace maladaptive behaviors with healthier ones. Nurses may implement step-by-step exposure or reward programs.

sections.title: Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy
sections.content: Cognitive-behavioral therapy combines behavior modification with cognitive restructuring to challenge irrational beliefs. Homework assignments and skills practice encourage clients to test new thinking patterns. CBT is evidence-based for many disorders.

sections.title: Humanistic Approach
sections.content: Humanistic approaches, such as Rogers’ person-centered therapy, view people as capable of growth when treated with empathy and unconditional positive regard. The therapist acts as a genuine, nonjudgmental partner.

sections.title: Milieu Therapy
sections.content: Milieu therapy uses the treatment environment as a therapeutic agent. Staff model healthy communication, set consistent expectations, and involve clients in decision making. Structured activities and peer support foster safety and community.

sections.title: Integrating Models in Nursing Practice
sections.content: Most nurses blend ideas from different models. They might use CBT worksheets while maintaining a supportive, humanistic stance. Integrating theories allows flexible, patient-centered care.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.content: Knowledge of major theories equips nurses to select interventions suited to each client’s needs and stage of recovery. Continued practice and reflection strengthen therapeutic relationships and outcomes.
